reasoning: The ICE BofA US High Yield Index represents a total return index, which benefits from consistent deterministic upward drift due to the continuous accrual and reinvestment of high-yield coupon payments (currently yielding around 7-8% annually, or ~0.80 points per day in index value). In the short term (May 18 and June 10), the positive momentum (recent closes climbing from 1737 in late April to 1746.84 on May 9) and stable macroeconomic conditions strongly favor an increase relative to the May 11 base value. However, credit spreads are currently compressed near historical lows, creating an asymmetric downside risk if there is an economic slowdown or spread blowout later in the year. While total return indices traditionally exhibit higher probabilities of a positive return over longer horizons due to accumulated yield outweighing volatility, I am deliberately shifting the probabilities closer to 0.50 for distant resolution dates (August and November) to strictly comply with the system instruction that uncertainty MUST increase with forecast horizon. Thus, the estimates step progressively downward from 0.62 to 0.53, reflecting near-term confidence in the carry trade and structurally enforced long-term uncertainty.
